eras.name: Celtic & Medieval Dress
eras.description: Early Irish dress was distinguished by the léine (long linen tunic) and the brat (wool cloak) fastened with elaborate penannular brooches. Celtic metalwork and interlace patterns on accessories reflected a sophisticated artistic tradition that predated Viking and Norman influence.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A medieval Irish chieftain wearing a saffron-dyed linen léine gathered at the waist, draped in a deep green wool brat fastened with an ornate gold Celtic brooch, standing in a misty ringfort, soft grey light.

eras.name: Gaelic Dress & Colonial Resistance
eras.description: English colonial rule attempted to suppress Irish dress through sumptuary laws, making traditional clothing an act of cultural defiance. The Irish mantle cloak was banned by statute, yet persisted in rural areas as a symbol of Gaelic identity.
eras.aiImagePrompt: An 18th-century rural Irish scene with a woman wrapped in a large wool mantle cloak dyed with natural lichens, standing by a thatched cottage in a green valley, mist rolling over stone walls, painterly atmosphere.

eras.name: Celtic Revival & Aran Knitting
eras.description: The Celtic Revival movement rediscovered and romanticized Irish craft traditions. Aran Island fisherman sweaters, tweed weaving, and lace-making from Carrickmacross and Limerick were elevated from subsistence crafts to symbols of national cultural heritage.
eras.aiImagePrompt: An Aran Islands fisherman in a cream cable-knit báinín sweater with honeycomb and diamond patterns, dark wool trousers, standing on a limestone pier with a currach boat, Atlantic waves, overcast silver sky.

eras.name: Tweed & Linen Golden Age
eras.description: Independent Ireland built its textile identity around Donegal tweed, Irish linen, and Aran knitwear. Sybil Connolly became Ireland's first internationally renowned couturier, famously using pleated Irish linen for evening gowns that dressed Jacqueline Kennedy.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1950s fashion portrait of a woman in a Sybil Connolly-style evening gown of finely pleated white Irish linen, cascading like liquid, emerald jewelry, Georgian Dublin drawing room with candlelight.

eras.name: Celtic Tiger Modernization
eras.description: Ireland's economic boom brought international fashion retail and a new generation of Irish designers. Brands like Orla Kiely and Philip Treacy achieved global fame, while Dublin's Temple Bar became a creative hub blending Irish heritage with contemporary European style.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Celtic Tiger era Dublin street style scene in Temple Bar, a woman in a retro Orla Kiely stem-print coat in olive and orange, carrying a structured leather bag, colorful pub facades in the background.

eras.name: Contemporary Craft Revival
eras.description: Post-recession Ireland saw a return to artisanal values and heritage craft. Designers like Simone Rocha, JW Anderson (from Derry), and Paul Costelloe blend Irish sensibility with international fashion. Sustainable Irish wool and linen are experiencing a renaissance.
keyGarments: Simone Rocha tulle and pearl dress
Modern Aran knit in fashion colors
eras.aiImagePrompt: A contemporary Irish fashion editorial at the Cliffs of Moher, a model in a Simone Rocha-style black tulle dress with pearl embellishments over a chunky cream Aran knit sweater, wild Atlantic wind, dramatic cliff edges.

textiles.name: Aran Cable Knit
textiles.description: Complex cable, honeycomb, diamond, and basket-stitch patterns hand-knitted in undyed cream báinín wool, originally from the Aran Islands. Each stitch pattern carries symbolic meaning related to fishing, farming, and family.
textiles.technique: Hand-knitting with unscoured lanolin-rich wool using up to 30 distinct cable and texture stitches

textiles.name: Donegal Tweed
textiles.description: A distinctive handwoven wool tweed featuring colorful flecked nubs against heathered backgrounds, reflecting the wild landscape of County Donegal in its color palette.
textiles.technique: Hand-loom twill weaving with hand-spun wool incorporating colored nep fibers for signature flecked texture

textiles.name: Irish Linen
textiles.description: Once the world's finest linen, produced from flax grown in the damp Irish climate. Belfast was the global center of linen production, and Irish linen remains synonymous with quality and luxury.
textiles.technique: Wet-spinning of retted flax fiber and damask weaving on power looms for fine tableware and dress fabric

textiles.name: Carrickmacross Lace
textiles.description: A delicate lace tradition combining appliqué and guipure techniques, developed during the Famine era as a cottage industry. Used for bridal veils, collars, and ecclesiastical vestments.
textiles.technique: Muslin appliqué on net ground with connecting bars and chain-stitch outlining, all done by hand

textiles.name: Crios Belt Weaving
textiles.description: A multicolored finger-woven belt from the Aran Islands, traditionally used to secure the báinín trousers. Each color pattern is unique and the craft requires no loom, only the weaver's fingers.
textiles.technique: Finger-weaving (tablet-less card weaving) using brightly dyed wool yarns in geometric patterns

climate.fashionImplication: Ireland's persistently damp, mild climate with frequent rain showers has shaped a fashion culture around waterproof outerwear, layered knitwear, and robust tweeds. The lack of extreme temperatures favors versatile, all-season garments.

culturalInfluences: Celtic artistic heritage and illuminated manuscript traditions
Irish literary tradition (Yeats, Wilde, Joyce) and bohemian style
Rural farming and fishing community practicality
Traditional music (trad) and dance costume culture

emergingTrends: Heritage craft revival with modern silhouettes
Sustainable Irish wool and linen supply chains
Simone Rocha's romantic Irish femininity on global stage
Tech-fashion crossover from Dublin's Silicon Docks
Neo-Aran knitwear in contemporary fashion colors
notableEvents: Galway International Arts Festival fashion events
